content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES Co1’iivIIssION
P.O. Box 807
. 428 J Street
. Sacramento. CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660
. Fax (916) 322-0886
January 12, 1999
John D. Jones
Board of Pharmacy
3562 Redwood Street
Irvine, California 92606
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-98-260
Dear Mr. Jones:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’ You are a member of the state Board of Pharmacy and also an
employee of a pharmacy benefits management company.
QUESTION
May you receive payment from pharmaceutical companies for your participation on
market research advisory panels?
CONCLUSION
You may. The pharmaceutical companies’ payment to you for your participation on the
panels is earned income, not prohibited honoraria. You must report the receipt of this income on
your Statement of Economic Interests. Accordingly, this income could become a disqualifying
source of income under certain circumstances. This could preclude you from making,
participating in making, or influencing certain decisions in your capacity as a member of the
Board of Phannacy.
Government Code sections 81000
- 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109
-
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. A-98-260
Page 2
FACTS
Governor Wilson appointed you to the Board of Pha.rmacy in June of 1998. You work
for Prescription Solutions, a pharmacy benefits management company which is a subsidiary of
PacifiCare Health Systems, a health maintenance organization that operates in California and
eight other western states. You are responsible for strategic planning to manage the drug benefits
for five million of your members. Almost three million of your members are Californians. Your
employer encourages you and others on your executive team to stay informed concerning the
research, development and marketing plans of the pharmaceutical industry.
The pharmaceutical industry routinely conducts market research advisory panels using
representatives from managed care companies for which they offer consulting fees to compensate
for panel participation and the time taken away from work. Most of the panels are conducted on
the weekend or necessitate taking a day off from work. The panel members are generally asked
to discuss how managed care companies will view the pharmaceutical company’s products and
comment on the lilcelthood that the products would be added to a health plan’s drug formulary.
The consulting pharmacists are encouraged to be blunt as to the merits of the drugs to their health
plans. The value of the panel to the pharmaceutical company is to gain honest and open
information regarding the managed care market prior to a new product launch.
Your employer has forwarded numerous requests for attendance on the advisory panels to
you with the knowledge that ifyou attend, it will not be on company time. Your employer
receives these requests, and forwards them randomly to several employees within your
organization. There is a benefit to your employer in that most panels are scheduled outside of
regular work hours and that there is no budgetary impact since the pharmaceutical company pays
all fees and expenses. Another benefit to your employer is the information gained by those
employees who attend the panels. The pharmaceutical companies often discuss their marketing
plans for new drugs, their research and development capabilities, their new product pipelines and
business initiatives. This intelligence is a major reason that managed care employers encourage
their pharmacists to participate in panels outside ofwork time. Finally, your employer benefits
throughthe information you convey to the panel sponsors regarding your business position and
philosophy on dealing with new drugs. There are more than ten pharmacists in your company
who attend the panels as they occur throughout the year.
You have participated in advisory panels for the past eight years. You have attended
between three and five panels per year. The pharmaceutical companies pay for panelist
transportation and overnight accommodations (if travel is required) for the meeting. They also
provide consulting fees that are equivalent to the time spent at the meeting. In other words, if
you spend a day participating in a panel, you will be reimbursed approximately equal to one
day’s pay from Prescription Solutions. You declare the income from the panels as earned
income under the designation of consulting fees for income tax purposes. You are required to
report the receipt of income or gifts from pharmaceutical companies on your Statement of
Economic Interests.


File No. A-98-260
Page 3
ANALYSIS
Honoraria Ban
Section 89502(c) provides that no member of a state board or commission shall accept an
honorarium from any source if the member would be required to report the receipt of income or
gifts from that source on his or her statement of economic interests. An “honorarium” is defined
as any payment made in consideration for any speech given, article published, or attendance at
any public or private conference, convention, meeting, social event, meal, or like gathering.
(Section 89501(a).) Regulation 18931.1 provides that “speech given” means “a public address,
oration, or other form of oral presentation, and includes participation in apanel, seminar, or
debate.” (Emphasis added.) Payment to you for your participation in the phannaceutical panels
is an honorarium. You may not accept the payment unless an exception to the ban applies.
The only exception that may apply under your facts is the earned income exception of
Section 89501(b) and Regulation 18932. Section 89501(b) states that an honorarium does not
include earned income.
2 The regulation further states, in pertinent part:
“(a) “Honorarium” does not include income earned for personal
services if:
(1) The services are provided in connection with an individual’s
business or the individual’s practice of or employment in a bona fide
business, trade, or profession, such as teaching, practicing law, medicine,
insurance, real estate, banking, or building contracting pursuant to
California Code of Regulations, Title 2, Sections 18932.1 through
18932.3; and
(2) The services are customarily provided in connection with the
business, trade, or profession.”
Your employer, Prescription Solutions, is certainly a bona fide business.
3 Your employer
receives the requests to attend the panels, your employer forwards the requests to employees on a
random basis, your employer encourages attendance at the panels, and you have outlined the
many ways in which employees’ participation benefit the company. Therefore, we conclude that
the services you are providing on the panel are “in connection with” your “employment in a bona
fide business.” Because other employees customarily attend the panels on behalf of Prescriptions
Solutions, we also conclude that your service on the panels are “customarily provided in
connection with” the pharmacy benefits management business. Accordingly, the pharmaceutical
2 Section 82030.5 defmes earned income and states, in part, “(a) For purposes of this title, ‘earned income’
means, except as provided in subdivision (b), income from wages, salaries, professional fees, and other amounts
received or promised tobe received as compensation for personal services rendered.”
A business is presumed to be “bona fide” if the owner of the business has maintained certain accounting,
bank and tax records over specific lengths of time. (See Regulation 18932.1, copy enclosed.) However, a business
whose predominant activity is making speeches is not “bona fide.” (Regulation 18932.1(d).)


File No. A-98-260
Page 4
companies’ payment to you for your participation on the panels is earned income, not prohibited
honoraria. As you indicated, you must report the receipt of this income on your Statement of
Economic Interests.
4
Travel/Gifts
Public officials are prohibited under the Act from accepting gifts from any single source
totaling more than $300 in a calendar year. (Section 89503.) Travel payments are usually
considered to be a gift. The term “travel payment” includes payments, advnes, or
reimbursements for travel, including actual transportation and related lodging and subsistence.
(Section 8950 1(c).) The Act provides exceptions to the gift limit and honoraria prohibition for
certain types of travel payments. (Section 89506; Regulations 18950-18950.4.) The
pharmaceutical companies pay for your transportation and accommodations to participate on
their panels. I therefore encourage you to familiarize yourselfwith the rules related to gifts and
travel under the Act. I enclose a pamphlet on “Gifts, Honoraria and Travel” for your review.
Incompatible Activities
Lastly, this letter addresses only whether accepting payment for your services on the
pharmaceutical panels would be prohibited honoraria under the Political Reform Act. It does not
address other state laws outside our jurisdiction which may preclude you from accepting these
payments. (See Section 1090 et seq., “Incompatible Activities of State Officers and
Employees.”) Your agency may have an internal policy disallowing payments to you from these
sources.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.
Sincerely,
Churchwell
General Co
s
By:
Deborah Allison
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
SGC:DA:tls
Please be aware that if you are asked to participate on a panel because of your status as a public official,
the exception discussed in this letter would not apply. In addition, income received by you from the pharmaceutical
companies could create a disqualifying conflict of interest in the future. In other words, your receipt of money may
preclude you from later making, participating in making, or influencing certain governmental decision in your
capacity as a member of the Board of Pharmacy. I enclose a copy of the conflict of interest regulations for your
review.

Board of Phannacy.Page 2
